1: The three-year famine is traced to Saul's unresolved bloodguilt against the Gibeonites: ʾel šāʾūl wỽʾel bît hadāmîm ʾal ʾăšer hēmît ʾet haggibʾōnîm. The Gibeonites were covenant partners with Israel from the days of Joshua (Josh 9:15-27) — an irrevocable bỽrît sworn in YHWH's name. Saul's massacre violated an oath sworn to YHWH, and the land bears the guilt of the unresolved covenant violation a generation later. The theological principle that unresolved bloodguilt (dāmîm) pollutes the land is grounded in Num 35:33: 'bloodshed pollutes the land, and no atonement can be made for the land for the blood that is shed in it, except by the blood of the one who shed it.' Covenant violations do not dissolve with time but await resolution. The NT carries this logic into the atonement: the accumulated bloodguilt of humanity requires not the passage of time but the shedding of the blood of the One in whom all covenant violations converge (Heb 9:22: 'without the shedding of blood there is no forgiveness').
17: After David nearly dies in battle, his men vow: lŏʼ tēṣʾēʼ ʾwôd ʾittanū lammilḥāmāh wỽlŏʼ tỽkabbîh ʾet nēr yiśrāʾēl — 'you shall not go out with us to battle anymore, lest you quench the lamp (nēr) of Israel.' The phrase nēr yiśrāʾēl (lamp of Israel) is the narrative's first explicit royal metaphor for the king as the source of national light and continuity. The nēr metaphor for the Davidic dynasty appears in 1 Kgs 11:36, 15:4, and 2 Kgs 8:19 — the lamp that YHWH has kept burning in Jerusalem as the sign of covenantal continuity. In the prophetic tradition, the light-bringer becomes explicitly messianic: Isa 9:2 ('the people who walked in darkness have seen a great light') and 42:6 ('I will make you a light for the nations'). Jesus's self-identification as the Light of the world (John 8:12) is the ultimate declaration that the nēr yiśrāʾēl motif reaches its fulfillment not in any son of David who can be killed in battle, but in the Son of David whose light is inextinguishable.
2: Va-yiqra ha-melekh la-Giv'onim vayomer aleyhem — David summoned the Gibeonites. The Gibeonites were not part of Israel but of the remnant of the Amorites (ha-Emori); Israel had sworn to spare them (Josh 9:15–20), but Saul had tried to destroy them “in his zeal for Israel and Judah.” The failure to name Saul's specific act may reflect lost narrative or deliberate vagueness; what matters legally is that a shevuat YHWH (oath of YHWH) had been violated.
3: Vayomer David el-ha-Giv'onim: mah e'eseh lakhem u-vammah achapper — David's question: “what shall I do for you and how shall I make atonement (achapper, Piel of kapar, to make atonement)?” The use of kipper for the remediation of the blood-guilt is significant: this is not merely political negotiation but covenantal atonement — the language of the cult applied to a civil-legal crisis. U-varekhu et-nachalat YHWH — that you may bless the inheritance of YHWH — David's goal is restoration of divine blessing over the land.
4: Vayomeru lo ha-Giv'onim: ein-lanu kesef ve-zahav im-Sha'ul ve-im-beito — the Gibeonites' refusal of silver and gold: ein-lanu (we have no claim for) — the rejection of financial compensation. Ve-ein-lanu ish le-hamit be-Yisra'el — they are not claiming general blood-vengeance. Their demand will be specific: particular lives of Saul's house for particular lives taken.
5: Vayomeru el-ha-melekh: ha-ish asher kilanu ve-asher dimmah-lanu nishmednu — “the man who consumed us and who devised to exterminate us.” Kilanu (Piel of kalah, consume, annihilate) and dimmah-lanu (devised against us, Piel of damah, plan) — the double verb of planned annihilation establishes the severity of Saul's crime. The crime was calculated (dimmah), making the blood-guilt especially serious.
6: Yuttan-lanu shiv'ah anashim mi-banav ve-hoqa'anum la-YHWH be-Giv'ah Sha'ul bachir YHWH — “let seven men of his sons be given to us, and we will impale them (hoqa'anum, Hiphil of yaqa', hang/impale/expose) before YHWH in Gibeah of Saul.” Hoqa' as a form of execution/exposure was used for the ultimate disgrace; it appears in Num 25:4 for the Baal-peor sinners. The number seven (shiv'ah) is the sacred completion number in ritual contexts.
7: Va-yachmol ha-melekh al-Mephiboshet ben-Yehonatan ben-Sha'ul — the king had compassion (yachmol, Qal of chamal, spare, have compassion) on Mephibosheth son of Jonathan. Al-shevuat YHWH asher beineyhem bein David u-vein Yehonatan ben-Sha'ul — because of the oath of YHWH between them, between David and Jonathan. The protection of Mephibosheth from the Gibeonite demand is the direct application of the Jonathan covenant (1 Sam 18:3, 20:15–17). David protects Mephibosheth not from general compassion but from specific covenant obligation.
8: Va-yiqach ha-melekh et-shenei venei Ritzpah bat-Ayah — the two sons of Rizpah: Armoni and Mephibosheth (a different Mephibosheth from Jonathan's son). Ve-et-chamisha venei Mikhal bat-Sha'ul — the MT reads Michal; 1 Sam 18:19 names Merab as Adriel's wife. The LXX and some manuscripts read Merab here, which is the lectio difficilior and more historically coherent reading.
9: Va-yitnem be-yad ha-Giv'onim va-yoqi'um ba-har lifnei YHWH — he gave them into the hands of the Gibeonites and they impaled them on the mountain before YHWH. Ve-hem humtu bi-ymei katzir ba-rishonim bi-tchillat katzir se'orim — they were put to death at the beginning of barley harvest. The harvest timing is significant: rain in the dry season was the divine sign (1 Sam 12:17) that YHWH's blessing was connected to covenant faithfulness.
10: Va-tiqach Ritzpah bat-Ayah et-ha-saq va-tattehu lah el-ha-tzur mi-tchillat katzir ad nittakh mayim aleihem min-ha-shamayim — Rizpah daughter of Aiah spread sackcloth on a rock for herself from the beginning of harvest until rain fell on them from heaven. The vigil of Rizpah — guarding the bodies of her sons from birds by day and beasts by night — is one of the most powerful maternal grief scenes in biblical literature. The sackcloth as mourning marker, her sustained presence as the guard that prevented the ultimate dishonor of the bodies being consumed.
11: Va-yuggad le-David et asher astha Ritzpah bat-Ayah pilegesh Sha'ul — the report of Rizpah's vigil triggers David's action: he goes to collect the bones of Saul and Jonathan from Jabesh-gilead. The narrative connection between Rizpah's vigil and David's retrieval creates the theological point: proper burial that honors the dead is the human response that completes the atonement begun by the impalement.
12: Va-yelekh David va-yiqach et-atzmot Sha'ul ve-et-atzmot Yehonatan beno me'et ba'alei Yavesh Gil'ad — David retrieved the bones of Saul and Jonathan from the men of Jabesh-gilead, who had taken them from the wall of Beth-shan (1 Sam 31:11–13). The recovery honors both Saul (the anointed king David respected throughout his life) and Jonathan (the covenant friend). The care shown for proper burial of both enemies and friends reflects the ancient Near Eastern understanding that burial was a sacred obligation.
13: Va-ya'al mi-sham et-atzmot Sha'ul ve-et-atzmot Yehonatan beno va-ya'aseph et-atzmot ha-muqa'im — he brought up the bones of Saul and Jonathan and gathered the bones of the impaled men. The gathering of all the bones together — Saul, Jonathan, and the seven impaled men — for burial in the ancestral tomb formally closes the blood-guilt account. The reburial in the ancestral tomb restores the honor of the royal Saulide line after the public execution.
14: Va-yiqberu et-atzmot Sha'ul u-Yehonatan beno be-eretz Binyamin be-Tzel'ah be-qever Qish avi Sha'ul — burial in Benjamin, in Zela, in the grave of Kish. The geographic specification (eretz Binyamin) returns the Saulide bones to their tribal territory and ancestral burial ground. Va-ye'ater Elohim la-aretz acharei-khen — and God was moved by entreaty (ya'ater, Niphal of atar, be entreated, be appeased) for the land after that. The verb marks the resolution of the divine anger — the famine ends because the blood-guilt has been properly atoned.
15: Ve-thi od ha-milchamah la-Pelishtim el-Yisra'el va-yered David ve-avadav imo va-yilachamu et-ha-Pelishtim — return of Philistine war. Va-yiyga'ph David — and David became weary (yaga', to be exhausted, weary). The notice of David's weariness in battle is the first explicit statement of his physical limitations — marking the transition from David as warrior-king to David as commander-king who cannot personally fight.
16: Ve-Yishbi-Benov asher bi-yelide ha-Rapha — Ishbi-benob who was among the descendants of the Rapha (ha-Rapha, the giant, the Rephaim). The Rephaim tradition — a pre-Israelite race of warriors of exceptional physical size — appears throughout the conquest narratives (Deut 3:11, Josh 12:4). Va-yachashov lehakot et-David — he thought (chashav, calculate) to strike David — a deliberate targeted attack on the king personally.
18: Va-tehi acharei-khen od ha-milchamah be-Gov im-ha-Pelishtim az hikkah Sibbechai ha-Chushati et-Saph asher bi-yelide ha-Rapha — at Gob, Sibbechai the Hushathite killed Saph, another Rephaim descendant. The series of giant-killings (vv. 18–22) — Sibbechai, Elhanan, Jonathan ben-Shimeah — documents the systematic elimination of Philistine warrior-heroes. Each killing is attributed to one of David's mighty men (gibborim), not to David himself — the transition from royal warrior to patron of warriors is complete.
19: Va-tehi od ha-milchamah be-Gov im-ha-Pelishtim va-yakh Elhanan ben-Ya'are-Oregim ha-Beit-ha-Lachmi et Golyat ha-Gitti — Elhanan killed Goliath the Gittite. The text as it stands attributes a Goliath-killing to Elhanan, which creates the famous tension with 1 Sam 17 (where David kills Goliath). The parallel in 1 Chr 20:5 reads “the brother of Goliath” — a text-critical variant that attempts to resolve the apparent contradiction. Whether the chronicle is a correction or preserves a different tradition is debated.
20: Va-tehi od milchamah be-Gat va-yehi ish middon ve-etzba'otav shesh ve-shesh esrim ve-arba — at Gath, a man of great stature with six fingers on each hand and six toes on each foot, twenty-four digits total. Va-yiledh gam-hu le-ha-Rapha — he also was born to the Rapha. The precise enumeration of digits (shesh ve-shesh esrim ve-arba) is a narrative curiosity that the text records with ethnographic precision — the warrior's physical anomaly as a marker of Rephaim descent.
21: Va-yecharaph et-Yisra'el va-yakkehhu Yehonatan ben-Shime'ah achi David — and when he taunted Israel, Jonathan son of Shimeah, David's brother, killed him. The verb charaph (taunt, reproach, defy) connects the Gittite giant to the Goliath narrative (1 Sam 17:10, 25–26, 36, 45 — the same root throughout): the taunting of Israel as a religious insult that required a champion to answer.
22: Arbah elleh yuldu la-Rapha ve-Gat va-yippelu be-yad David u-ve-yad avadav — “these four were born to the Rapha in Gath and they fell by the hand of David and by the hand of his servants.” The summary formula — yippelu be-yad David u-ve-yad avadav — is the key closing statement: the victories belong to both David and his servants. The aging king who can no longer fight personally is credited with the victories won by his men — the covenant community's victories belong to its anointed head.
